questions.q: What kind of device is the Jaipur Foot?
questions.explanation: The Jaipur Foot is a rubber-based prosthetic limb that restores mobility to people who have lost a leg or foot.

questions.q: The Jaipur Foot is named after which Indian city?
questions.explanation: The prosthesis takes its name from Jaipur, the Rajasthan city where it was developed.

questions.q: What is the Jaipur Foot most famous for being?
options: The most expensive prosthetic in the world
A purely cosmetic device that cannot bear weight
questions.explanation: Its great achievement is providing a rugged, functional artificial limb at very low cost, making it affordable to the poor.

questions.q: In which country was the Jaipur Foot developed?
questions.explanation: The Jaipur Foot was designed and developed in India, in the city of Jaipur.

questions.q: Who are the main beneficiaries of the Jaipur Foot?
options: Amputees who have lost a limb
questions.explanation: The Jaipur Foot is designed for amputees, restoring their ability to walk after losing a leg or foot.

questions.q: Roughly how many people are said to have benefited from the Jaipur Foot worldwide?
questions.explanation: The device has restored mobility to millions of amputees in India and around the world.

questions.q: What everyday flexible material is central to the design of the Jaipur Foot?
questions.explanation: The Jaipur Foot is built largely from rubber, giving it flexibility and durability at low cost.

questions.q: A key practical advantage of the Jaipur Foot for users in India is that it allows them to do what?
options: Walk barefoot and squat on uneven ground
questions.explanation: Its rugged, flexible design lets users walk barefoot, squat, and move on rough or uneven terrain common in rural India.

questions.q: Compared with imported Western prosthetic limbs, the Jaipur Foot is generally:
questions.explanation: The Jaipur Foot is dramatically cheaper than conventional Western prostheses, which is the heart of its impact.

questions.q: The Jaipur Foot is best described as an example of which kind of innovation?
options: Frugal, affordable engineering for social good
questions.explanation: It is a classic example of frugal innovation: a simple, cheap, effective solution to a major human need.

questions.q: Which Indian craftsman is widely credited as the master technician who, together with a doctor, created the Jaipur Foot?
questions.explanation: Master craftsman Ram Chandra Sharma developed the rubber foot's design alongside the orthopaedic surgeon.

questions.q: Which orthopaedic surgeon is credited as the medical force behind the Jaipur Foot?
questions.explanation: Orthopaedic surgeon Dr. Pramod Karan Sethi worked with Ram Chandra Sharma to develop the Jaipur Foot.

questions.q: In which decade was the Jaipur Foot first developed?
questions.explanation: The Jaipur Foot was developed in the late 1960s, around 1968, in Jaipur.

questions.q: Dr. Pramod Karan Sethi received which major Indian award in 1981, in part for the Jaipur Foot work?
questions.explanation: Dr. P. K. Sethi was honoured with the Ramon Magsaysay Award in 1981 for the Jaipur Foot's social impact.

questions.q: Which charitable organisation is most associated with manufacturing and distributing the Jaipur Foot free of charge?
options: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research
questions.explanation: The Jaipur-based BMVSS organisation has long fitted Jaipur Feet to amputees free of charge.

questions.q: Why was the Jaipur Foot especially suited to Indian conditions compared with rigid foreign designs?
options: It allowed barefoot walking, squatting and sitting cross-legged
It only worked on paved roads
questions.explanation: Its flexibility let users walk barefoot, squat and sit cross-legged, matching everyday Indian life and worship.

questions.q: A notable feature of the Jaipur Foot's design is that it is:
options: Fully waterproof and lightweight enough for fieldwork and wet ground
Powered by a small petrol engine
questions.explanation: It is waterproof and rugged, so users can work in paddy fields, mud and water, unlike many imported limbs.

questions.q: The Jaipur Foot was deliberately designed so that it could be fitted to a patient in a very short time. Roughly how long does fitting typically take?
options: About a single day or even hours
questions.explanation: A major advantage is that a Jaipur Foot can often be made and fitted within a day, sometimes in just hours.

questions.q: The colour and appearance of the Jaipur Foot were chosen so that it would:
options: Resemble a natural human foot in shape and skin tone
questions.explanation: It was moulded to look like a natural foot, helping users feel less self-conscious and walk barefoot.

questions.q: Which of these best describes the Jaipur Foot's intellectual-property approach?
options: It was patented and sold at premium prices
Its design was kept open and made widely accessible to maximise reach
It was licensed only to one foreign company
questions.explanation: Its creators prioritised affordability and broad access over profit, allowing the design to spread widely.

questions.q: The Jaipur Foot programme has carried out fitment camps not only in India but also in many other:
options: Developing countries across Asia, Africa and Latin America
questions.explanation: BMVSS has held camps in numerous developing countries, extending the Jaipur Foot's reach worldwide.

questions.q: What problem with traditional Western prosthetic feet did the Jaipur Foot specifically aim to overcome for Indian users?
options: They required wearing shoes and could not handle squatting or uneven ground
They were too light to feel
They worked only in cold climates
questions.explanation: Western SACH feet needed shoes and rigid gait; the Jaipur Foot allowed barefoot, flexible movement on rough terrain.

questions.q: The Jaipur Foot is often contrasted with which standard Western prosthetic foot design?
options: The SACH (Solid Ankle Cushion Heel) foot
questions.explanation: Western prosthetics commonly used the rigid SACH foot, whereas the Jaipur Foot offered greater flexibility and barefoot use.

questions.q: In which year is the Jaipur Foot generally said to have been developed?
questions.explanation: The Jaipur Foot is traditionally dated to 1968, created by Ram Chandra Sharma and Dr. P. K. Sethi.

questions.q: The Jaipur Foot's design allows a flexible ankle joint that mimics which natural foot motions?
questions.explanation: Its rubber construction permits up-down and side-to-side ankle movement, closely imitating a real foot.

questions.q: Beyond rubber, the Jaipur Foot's body has traditionally incorporated which inexpensive locally available materials?
options: Wood and cushioning materials with a rubber exterior
questions.explanation: The classic Jaipur Foot combined a wooden keel and cushioning with vulcanised rubber, all cheap and locally sourced.

questions.q: The Jaipur Foot has been recognised internationally; it has notably been featured or supported in collaborations with which kind of institutions?
options: Leading engineering and research institutions seeking to refine its materials
questions.explanation: Engineering and research bodies have collaborated with the Jaipur Foot programme to improve materials and design.

questions.q: What social impact, beyond physical mobility, is most often attributed to the Jaipur Foot?
options: It restored dignity and the ability to earn a livelihood for amputees
questions.explanation: By restoring the ability to walk and work, the Jaipur Foot returned independence, dignity and earning power to amputees.

questions.q: Which statement about the durability of the Jaipur Foot is accurate?
options: It must be discarded after a single use
It is rugged enough to withstand years of hard daily use in rough conditions
It can only be used indoors on carpet
questions.explanation: The Jaipur Foot was engineered to be tough and long-lasting, surviving mud, water and demanding rural labour.

questions.q: The Jaipur Foot is celebrated as an example of which broader Indian contribution to global technology?
options: Affordable, humane biomedical innovation accessible to the poor
questions.explanation: It stands as a landmark of low-cost, socially driven biomedical innovation that reached the world's poorest amputees.
